fig01: Grouped bar chart comparing four methods (Baseline, Method-A, Method-B, and OursModel) across five benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, MATH, GPQA). Error bars show sample standard deviation over 5 seeds. OursModel (dark orange, \texttt{xxx} hatch) achieves the highest accuracy on all five benchmarks. Palette chosen for colour-blind accessibility (Okabe--Ito); hatch patterns additionally distinguish methods in greyscale.
fig02: Validation loss over 50 training steps for three methods (mean $\pm$ 95\% CI across $n{=}5$ seeds, log-scale $y$-axis). \textbf{Ours} converges faster and achieves the lowest final loss; the double-headed arrow marks the gap $\Delta{=}0.155$ between Ours and the next-best competitor (Method-A) at the final step.
fig03: Chinchilla-style scaling law: validation loss vs.\ model size (log\textsubscript{10} scale) across 80 models. The red OLS regression line has slope $= -0.0449$, intercept $= 0.6209$, and $R^2 = 0.877$, indicating that $\sim\!88\%$ of variance in validation loss is explained by parameter count. The highlighted point (OursModel) is the data point nearest to $(\log_{10}N, \mathcal{L}) = (9.4,\,0.205)$, corresponding to $(9.449,\,0.210)$.
fig04:
\textbf{Method overview and empirical evaluation.}
\textbf{(A)}~Conceptual diagram of the three-stage pipeline (Encode\,\textrightarrow\,Attend\,\textrightarrow\,Decode) with memory and skill feedback loops.
\textbf{(B)}~Ablation study showing score (\%) when each component is removed; the full model (\textit{full}) achieves 64.7\%, with the largest drop from removing \textit{--mem} (55.1\%).
\textbf{(C)}~Training loss curves over 30k steps; \textit{Ours} converges faster and reaches a lower final loss (0.165) than the \textit{Baseline} (0.360).
\textbf{(D)}~Sensitivity sweep across parameters \(S_{0\text{--}4}\) and \(P_{0\text{--}4}\); colour encodes normalised score (0--1), revealing high-performance regions near \(S_3\)--\(S_4\).
